case_name: Chlortetracycline removal from groundwater for small drinking water system
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for chlortetracycline-contaminated groundwater intended for drinking water supply under low-energy small-system constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small rural groundwater utility has detected chlortetracycline in a production well influenced by nearby agricultural activity. The system needs a practical treatment approach that can reliably reduce the antibiotic to a very low level for drinking water use. Operators are limited in staffing and prefer compact, low-energy, proven treatment processes over advanced high-energy systems.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve strong removal of chlortetracycline for drinking water production while maintaining stable general water quality and low operational burden.
secondary_goals: use established treatment approaches with practical replacement or regeneration needs

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: limited residuals handling capacity; periodic spent media replacement is acceptable but large sludge or brine streams are undesirable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: reliable operation with simple monitoring and stable performance is required

core_unit_functions.pre_filtration: remove incidental particulates and protect downstream adsorptive media
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb chlortetracycline and other trace organic micropollutants from groundwater
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain carbon fines and provide final polishing
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: chlortetracycline is an organic micropollutant for which activated carbon is a well-established control approach
the raw groundwater has low turbidity and relatively low DOC, which supports effective adsorption capacity
GAC is compatible with small drinking water systems and has low direct energy demand
the process train is more practical than high-energy membrane or oxidation systems under the stated constraints
potential_risks: media exhaustion or breakthrough can occur if bed life is not tracked
natural organic matter competes for adsorption sites and can reduce run length
spent carbon replacement or reactivation logistics must be planned

core_unit_functions.pre_filtration: stabilize influent solids loading before adsorption contact
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: provide adsorptive removal of chlortetracycline in a contact basin or tank
core_unit_functions.cartridge_or_media_filtration: separate PAC particles and polish the water

applicability_rationale: PAC is also a recognized adsorptive option for trace organic compounds including pharmaceutical-like contaminants
it can be attractive where a small system prefers flexible chemical dosing rather than fixed-bed replacement intervals
energy demand remains low compared with pressure-driven membranes or advanced oxidation
the simple groundwater matrix makes adsorption-based treatment feasible
potential_risks: PAC dosing and solids separation require more active operator attention than fixed-bed GAC
carbon carryover or poor solids capture can affect finished water quality
ongoing carbon consumption may raise operating cost if contaminant episodes are prolonged

core_unit_functions.pre_filtration: protect membrane elements from fouling by fine solids
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_low_pressure_reverse_o: physically reject chlortetracycline and other dissolved constituents
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after mineral removal

applicability_rationale: membranes can provide strong removal of many antibiotics and trace organic contaminants
the groundwater quality is relatively clean and therefore technically suitable for membrane operation
this option may be considered when simultaneous reduction of multiple dissolved constituents is desired
potential_risks: concentrate management is difficult for a small system
energy demand is misaligned with the low-energy requirement
membrane scaling, fouling, and post-treatment needs increase complexity

core_unit_functions.oxidant_dosing: provide precursor oxidant for radical generation
core_unit_functions.uv_based_advanced_oxidation: degrade chlortetracycline through hydroxyl radical oxidation
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_carbon_or_filtration: remove transformation byproducts and polish treated water

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can degrade antibiotics that are otherwise persistent
this route can be effective when direct contaminant destruction is desired rather than phase transfer
post-polishing is useful because oxidation may form intermediate products
potential_risks: higher power and chemical demand conflict with the small-system low-energy setting
process control is more complex than adsorption systems
incomplete oxidation can create transformation products requiring further control

core_unit_functions.ion_exchange_or_standard_softening: attempt removal of dissolved constituents through exchange or precipitation mechanisms
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove any generated particles and polish effluent

applicability_rationale: this route is not a mainstream first-choice technology for chlortetracycline removal in drinking water
it may be explored only in niche cases with site-specific sorptive resin selection
standard softening is not strongly aligned with the stated single organic micropollutant problem
potential_risks: uncertain and less proven performance for chlortetracycline compared with activated carbon
resin fouling or regeneration waste may create operational burdens
technology selection may be unnecessarily indirect for this contaminant profile

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based_processes should rank above membrane and advanced oxidation options
fixed_bed_gac should be favored as the top recommendation for a small low-energy groundwater system with a single antibiotic contaminant
high-energy or high-complexity options should not rank first

must_reflect: low_energy_constraint should push adsorption ahead of membrane and uv_aop options
small_system preference should favor simple robust operation and manageable residuals
single_contaminant groundwater case does not justify unnecessarily complex multi-barrier high-energy treatment

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for trace organic micropollutants such as chlortetracycline
why low turbidity and modest DOC groundwater supports adsorption performance
why membranes and advanced oxidation rank lower under low-energy and small-system constraints
